bibliographic_data.title: BOtied: Multi-objective Bayesian optimization with tied multivariate ranks
bibliographic_data.abstract: Many scientific and industrial applications require the joint optimization of multiple, potentially competing objectives. Multi-objective Bayesian optimization (MOBO) is a sample-efficient framework for identifying Pareto-optimal solutions. At the heart of MOBO is the acquisition function, which determines the next candidate to evaluate by navigating the best compromises among the objectives. In this paper, we show a natural connection between non-dominated solutions and the extreme quantile of the joint cumulative distribution function (CDF). Motivated by this link, we propose the Pareto-compliant CDF indicator and the associated acquisition function, BOtied. BOtied inherits desirable invariance properties of the CDF, and an efficient implementation with copulas allows it to scale to many objectives. Our experiments on a variety of synthetic and real-world problems demonstrate that BOtied outperforms state-of-the-art MOBO acquisition functions while being computationally efficient for many objectives.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes BOtied, a novel acquisition function based on multivariate ranks that is invariant to relative rescaling or monotonic transformations of the objectives.
core_content.key_methodology: BOtied uses the joint cumulative distribution function (CDF) and its α-level lines to rank candidate solutions. The method leverages the invariance properties of the CDF to navigate trade-offs among multiple objectives efficiently.
outcomes: BOtied outperforms state-of-the-art MOBO acquisition functions
BOtied is computationally efficient for many objectives
core_content.full_text_markdown: Bayesian optimization (BO) has shown promise in optimizing expensive black-box functions with limited evaluations. However, when dealing with multiple competing objectives, the traditional single-objective approach falls short. This paper introduces BOtied, a novel acquisition function that addresses these challenges by leveraging multivariate ranks and the CDF. The authors demonstrate that BOtied is invariant to relative rescalings or monotonic transformations of the objectives, making it more robust for practical applications like biochemistry where unit conversions are common. Through experiments on both synthetic and real-world problems, they show that BOtied outperforms existing methods while maintaining computational efficiency.
